And even if we sometimes think we would run the world differently, in our limited understanding, we know so little with all we know. We still cannot answer most, if any, of the questions asked of Job many centuries ago, when the Lord God answered Job out of the whirlwind and said, [quoted Job 38] (Richard L. Evans, in Conference Report, Apr. 1957, 13; or Improvement Era, June 1957, 403)

A. ANOTHER CHALLENGE TO JOB (40:6-7) 1. As the Lord continues to answer Job out of the whirlwind 2. Job challenged to answer the questions God will ask him

What four questions does He then ask of Job? (40:8-9) - "Would you indeed annul My judgment?" - "Would you condemn Me that you may be justified?" (cf. 32:2) - "Have you an arm like God?" - "Can you thunder with a voice like His?"

What four questions does He then ask of Job? (40:8-9) - "Would you indeed annul My judgment?" - "Would you condemn Me that you may be justified?" (cf. 32:2) - "Have you an arm like God?" - "Can you thunder with a voice like His?"

What four questions does He then ask of Job? (40:8-9) - "Would you indeed annul My judgment?" - "Would you condemn Me that you may be justified?" (cf. 32:2) - "Have you an arm like God?" - "Can you thunder with a voice like His?"

What four questions does He then ask of Job? (40:8-9) - "Would you indeed annul My judgment?" - "Would you condemn Me that you may be justified?" (cf. 32:2) - "Have you an arm like God?" - "Can you thunder with a voice like His?"

What four questions does He then ask of Job? (40:8-9) - "Would you indeed annul My judgment?" - "Would you condemn Me that you may be justified?" (cf. 32:2) - "Have you an arm like God?" - "Can you thunder with a voice like His?"

bind them: literally, "bind their faces." There is some justification in the Bible for taking "face" as a synecdoche for "person." (Raymond P. Scheindlin, The Book of Job, W. W. Norton & Company New York, 1998, pp 220)

bind them: literally, "bind their faces." There is some justification in the Bible for taking "face" as a synecdoche for "person." (Raymond P. Scheindlin, The Book of Job, W. W. Norton & Company New York, 1998, pp 220)

matches.lb: Quoting Commentary: Ibn Ezra on Leviticus 26:4:2
matches.x: HER PRODUCE. The word yevulah (her produce) is related to the word vul (food) in Surely the mountains bring him forth food (Job 40:20). We do not know whether the yod in yevul is a root letter or is like the yod in yekum (living substance) (Gen.7:23),21From the root kof, vav, mem. which is not a root letter.
